coredata.dc:title: Highly conductive riboflavin-based carbon quantum dot–embedded SiO2@MoS2 nanocomposite for enhancing bioelectricity generation through synergistic direct and indirect electron transport
coredata.dc:creator: Kuo, Yi-Ho
Hsu, Ming-Chien
Wang, Wen-Jyun
Peng, Hung-Hsi
Li, Wei-Peng
coredata.dc:description: A microbial fuel cell (MFC) is an advanced green battery that has limited application because of its low current density. In the present study, Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, which is an electricity-producing bacterium, was used in an electrochemical reactor as a bacterial model. Mesoporous spherical silica nanoparticles (NPs) loaded with riboflavin (RF) were prepared as the starting material, following which surface modification was conducted to expose the thiol group, which considerably increased the affinity of the NPs to MoS2 and resulted in the formation of a dense MoS2 shell on the surface after calcination. Moreover, the loaded RFs were successfully transformed into RF-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which resulted in a substantial increase in conductivity. The CQD-embedded SiO2@MoS2 NPs, which contained redox-active N-doped CQDs and had a metallic MoS2 shell, were able to receive electrons from exoelectrogenic bacteria (charging) and transfer electrons to an indium tin oxide electrode (discharging). Thus, these NPs could act as an electron nanoshuttle and a conductive medium in biofilms for enhancing systematic extracellular electron transport. A 10-fold increase in bioelectricity production than no NPs addition was achieved, which confirmed the applicability of the aforementioned NPs in advanced MFC applications. The NPs prepared in this study, which mimic biological electron shuttles (e.g., RF) in long-distance conduction, can usher in a new era in the development of advanced MFCs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface thiolation and characterization of the mesoporous and RF-loaded SiO2 NPs. (a) Preparation of microbial electrochemically active SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs and their use in improving the performance of MFCs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the (b) mesoporous SiO2 NPs, (c) RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, (d) RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs, and (e) SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (f) Scanning electron microcopy image of the SiO2@MoS2 NPs. (g) Results of the BET analysis of the untreated SiO2 NPs, EtOH-washed SiO2 NPs, acid-treated SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, SiO2@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (h) Fourier-transform infrared spectra of RF, SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. Vibration peaks of S–H, Si–O, and Si–O–Si are located at 2560, 1250, and 1100 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic vibration peaks of CO (at 1650 and 1731 cm−1) and CC (at 1516 cm−1) are observed for RF, the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, and the RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs. Vibration peaks of Mo–O and Mo–S are located at 659 and 485 cm−1, respectively. (i) Pellets of SiO2 and RF-loaded SiO2 NPs after centrifugation. (j) UV–Vis spectra of RF and the SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, SiO2@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (k) Results of the thermogravimetric analysis of RF, CTAB, the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, and the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (l) Zeta potentials of the SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs determined through dynamic light scattering. (n.s., no significance; ** p < 0.01).

Characterization of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (a) TEM images of the SiO2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs0.2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs20@MoS2 NPs and (b) their respective size distributions. (c) Raman spectra of the SiO2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs0.2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs20@MoS2 NPs. These spectra depict the characteristic G and D bands contributed by CTAB and RF-based CQDs. (d) Results of energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analysis for SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs. (e) High-resolution TEM images of the MoS2 shell with additional lattice plane information. The TEM image with a red frame indicates the magnified region of the HR-TEM image. (f) X-ray diffraction pattern of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs. (g) Fluorescence spectra of the SiO2@MoS2 NPs and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs. Under extinction at 370 nm, emission from CQDs was detected at 400–550 nm. (h) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs. The 3 P3/2 binding energies of Mo(IV) and Mo(VI) are 394.3 and 396.9 eV, respectively, and the binding energy of the N–C bond is 400.0 eV. (i) CV measurements for the SiO2@MoS2 NPs and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs in a DM solution with a pH of 7.4. The CV comparison for both types of NPs is under the same particle number (Si concentration was fixed at 18 ppm). The Si concentration of NPs was determined by ICP-AES analysis (Thermo Fisher Scientific iCAP TQ, Germany). The scan rate was set as 20 mV/s, and measurement was conducted over 20 cycles. (j) Bacterial growth curve of S. oneidensis MR-1 with and without SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs at 30 ℃. An optical density of 600 nm was selected to evaluate cell growth.

Electrochemical and compositional analyses of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (a) CA profiles of S. oneidensis MR-1, the SiO2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2@MoS2 NPs + S. oneidensis MR-1, and MoS2 nanosheets + S. oneidensis MR-1. (b) CA profiles of S. oneidensis MR-1, S. oneidensis MR-1 ΔOmcA/MtrC, the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs + S. oneidensis MR-1, and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs + S. oneidensis MR-1 ΔOmcA/MtrC. (c) CA profiles and (d) CV measurements for S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2@MoS2 NPs, S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs0.2@MoS2 NPs, S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs, S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs20@MoS2 NPs. (e) DPV analysis of S. oneidensis MR-1, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs + S. oneidensis MR-1 at a pulsed potential range of − 0.1 to 0.4 V versus a Ag pseudo-reference electrode. The different types of electron sources, including direct EET by adhesive bacteria, direct EET by adhesive NP-bound bacteria, and long-distance EET by nanoshuttles, were indicated by blue, green, and red arrows, respectively. XPS analysis of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs (f) before and (g) after electrochemical treatment. For the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, the binding energies of Mo(IV, 3d5/2), Mo(IV, 3d3/2), Mo(VI, 3d5/2), Mo(VI, 3d3/2), and S were 228.81, 231.94, 233.15, 235.10, and 226 eV, respectively. All the electrochemical measurements were performed at an oxidation potential of 0.2 V (versus a Ag pseudo-reference electrode) and under anaerobic conditions.

Additional analyses of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs to explore the possible mechanism for the enhancement of EET. (a) TEM image and (b) XPS analysis of SiO2-CQDs(citrate)@MoS2 NPs. The 3 P3/2 binding energies of Mo(IV) and Mo(VI) are 394.7 and 397.2 eV, respectively. (c) CA profiles of S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs and of S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs(citrate)@MoS2 NPs under an oxidation potential of 0.2 V (versus a Ag pseudo-reference electrode) and anaerobic conditions. TEM images of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs with (d) thin and (e) thick MoS2 shells. (f) CA profiles of S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs with different MoS2 shell thicknesses. (g) Possible mechanism for the enhancement of EET by SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (VB: valence band, CB: conduction band).

Alternative electrochemical manipulation by using SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs. (a) Electrochemical CA measurements for S. oneidensis MR-1 with an ITO electrode, an ITO electrode with a thin coating of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs, and an ITO electrode with SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 nanoshuttles. (b) The fluorescence images of S. oneidensis MR-1 biofilm and NPs-doped biofilm on the working electrodes. The commercial DAPI dye was used to label the biofilms following a standard staining protocol. The fluorescence signals of DAPI-labeled biofilms with and without nanoshuttles were observed by the DAPI (em:432–482 nm) and FITC (em:512–558 nm) channels. The scale bar represents ten (μm). (c) CA profiles of S. oneidensis MR-1 + SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs with and without stirring every hour. The dotted lines indicate that stirring was performed during electrochemical measurement. The inserts show the dispersion condition of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 nanoshuttles inside the electrochemical reactor at each reaction time. (d) Reciprocal relationship between indirect EET (suspended SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 nanoshuttles) and direct EET (SiO2-CQDs@MoS2-mixed biofilm) in an electrochemical reactor for synergistically enhancing the overall EET.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A microbial fuel cell (MFC) is an advanced green battery that has limited application because of its low current density. In the present study, Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, which is an electricity-producing bacterium, was used in an electrochemical reactor as a bacterial model. Mesoporous spherical silica nanoparticles (NPs) loaded with riboflavin (RF) were prepared as the starting material, following which surface modification was conducted to expose the thiol group, which considerably increased the affinity of the NPs to MoS2 and resulted in the formation of a dense MoS2 shell on the surface after calcination. Moreover, the loaded RFs were successfully transformed into RF-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which resulted in a substantial increase in conductivity. The CQD-embedded SiO2@MoS2 NPs, which contained redox-active N-doped CQDs and had a metallic MoS2 shell, were able to receive electrons from exoelectrogenic bacteria (charging) and transfer electrons to an indium tin oxide electrode (discharging). Thus, these NPs could act as an electron nanoshuttle and a conductive medium in biofilms for enhancing systematic extracellular electron transport. A 10-fold increase in bioelectricity production than no NPs addition was achieved, which confirmed the applicability of the aforementioned NPs in advanced MFC applications. The NPs prepared in this study, which mimic biological electron shuttles (e.g., RF) in long-distance conduction, can usher in a new era in the development of advanced MFCs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A microbial fuel cell (MFC) is an advanced green battery that has limited application because of its low current density. In the present study, Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, which is an electricity-producing bacterium, was used in an electrochemical reactor as a bacterial model. Mesoporous spherical silica nanoparticles (NPs) loaded with riboflavin (RF) were prepared as the starting material, following which surface modification was conducted to expose the thiol group, which considerably increased the affinity of the NPs to MoS2 and resulted in the formation of a dense MoS2 shell on the surface after calcination. Moreover, the loaded RFs were successfully transformed into RF-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which resulted in a substantial increase in conductivity. The CQD-embedded SiO2@MoS2 NPs, which contained redox-active N-doped CQDs and had a metallic MoS2 shell, were able to receive electrons from exoelectrogenic bacteria (charging) and transfer electrons to an indium tin oxide electrode (discharging). Thus, these NPs could act as an electron nanoshuttle and a conductive medium in biofilms for enhancing systematic extracellular electron transport. A 10-fold increase in bioelectricity production than no NPs addition was achieved, which confirmed the applicability of the aforementioned NPs in advanced MFC applications. The NPs prepared in this study, which mimic biological electron shuttles (e.g., RF) in long-distance conduction, can usher in a new era in the development of advanced MFCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial fuel cells (MFCs) are renewable energy devices in which electricity-producing bacteria metabolize carbon sources, such as glucose and lactates, to release metabolic electrons into an extracellular anode [1]. The biodegradable organic pollutants in wastewater, spoiled food, and dirty exhaust contribute to the generation of power by MFCs; thus, MFCs are a promising and green technology for energy generation [2]. In addition, microbial electrolysis through MFCs is a technique used in carbon fixation and biofuel production [3]. MFCs are also used as biosensors to detect electrochemically active pathogens [4]. Two cell models, namely Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 and Geobacter, have been widely used in MFCs because of their excellent extracellular electron transport (EET) capacity [5]. In S. oneidensis MR-1, Mtr EET pathway involving inner membrane c-type cytochrome (c-Cyt) CymA, periplasmic Fcc3 and STC, outer membrane c-Cyt MtrCAB complex, and outer membrane c-Cyt OmcA protein play an essential role in electron transport across the bacterial cell envelope. Three EET pathways have been reported for the delivery of electrons by S. oneidensis MR-1 to extracellular acceptors: (1) direct electron transfer through membrane proteins MtrC or OmcA contact, (2) direct long-distance electron transfer through the outer membrane and periplasm extensions (conductive nanowire), and (3) indirect long-distance electron transport with the assistance of secreted redox molecules that function as electron shuttles [5,6] . Attached electrogenic bacteria use the first and second pathways to transfer electrons directly to the electrode, whereas suspended cells rely on conductive molecules such as flavin mononucleotide and riboflavin (RF), which are common cell-secreted electron shuttles, to engage in indirect EET. In general, the efficiency of EET is determined by the adhesive condition of the cells, the bacterial activity, the concentration of the electron donor and electron shuttles, and the type of anode used. Improving the electrode is the most common and feasible method for considerably enhancing the performance of EET and MFCs [7].

In general, the conductivity, surface area, biocompatibility, and stability of an anode must be considered before its use in MFCs [8]. Among these factors, conductivity and surface area are the most influential in electron transport and determine the efficiency of MFCs. Nanomaterials with high conductivity and a high specific surface area have attracted considerable interest for the development of novel electrodes [9]. However, poor biocompatibility might reduce bacterial activity and even induce cell death, which results in catastrophic effects on the capacity of MFCs to produce electricity. Moreover, electrode stability is the most fundamental requirement for MFCs to achieve stable power output and renewable use.

A list of studies that have used nanomaterials for enhancing EET is presented in Table S1. Almost studies utilized their nanomaterials to modify the general working electrodes to improve their conductivities and active surface areas [10–15]. Another type of nanomaterial implantation for MFCs is inoculation, achieved by pre-incubating nanomaterials and bacteria to form the biofilm, then integrating it with an electrode for bioelectricity acquirement [16,17]. According to the unique characteristics of MFCs containing living microorganisms and the EET effect in MFCs, electrode improvement and inoculation are not the absolute methods to increase the generated power. For example, S. oneidensis MR-1 generates electrically conductive nanowires to deliver electrons to electrodes [5,6,18]. Inspired by natural nanowires, a previous study proposed a method for preparing a conductive polymer with well-designed ion pairing for use as a conductive nanowire to enhance the long-distance EET between a bacterium and an electrode [19]. In another study, a conductive polymeric substance was used as a bacterial scaffold to improve the electron transport performance [20]. Typically, bacteria secrete natural electron shuttles, such as RF and outer-membrane vesicles, to increase indirect EET [5,6,21]. Other molecular or ionic electron shuttles, including neurotransmitters, polyphenolic compounds, and Cu(II) ions, have been applied to increase the bioelectricity of MFCs [22–24]. A study reported that redox-active magnetite nanoparticles (NPs) can act as a nanoscale electron shuttle between a coculture of Fe(II)-oxidizing and Fe(III)-reducing bacteria [25]. The NPs deposition on the bacterial surface has also been indicated to facilitate the mass transfer or electron transfer in extracellular substances [26–29].

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is an emerging two-dimensional material with a high surface area, strong surface ion adsorption, excellent electrical conductivity, and high stability during repeated charge and discharge cycles; thus, MoS2 has high potential in electrochemical applications [30]. 2 H- and 1 T-MoS2 exhibit semiconductor and metallic properties, respectively [31]. Because of the excellent conductivity, electrochemical stability, and biocompatibility of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), these nanomaterials have attracted substantial interest in numerous fields, such as material science, natural science, electronics, energy, and medicine [32]. Fluorescent CQDs with excellent biocompatibility have been used as cell probes [33]. A study achieved considerable enhancement in electron transport performance through the N-doping of CQDs [34]. Interestingly, feeding CQDs to exoelectrogenic bacteria considerably accelerates EET, metabolism, and biofilm formation, thereby resulting in a 6–7-fold increase in bioelectricity generation [35,36]. Nanocomposites with high conductivity and electrochemical stability are fabricated by combining MoS2 and carbon compositions, and these nanocomposites have high potential for use in MFC-related applications [37–39].

In the present study, MoS2/CQD-based nanocomposites were used in MFCs based on S. oneidensis MR-1 to fill the aforementioned research gap. Mesoporous silica NPs were prepared as the core, following which a MoS2 shell was coated on the NPs to form a yarn-ball-like nanocomposite. N-doped CQDs were then successfully embedded into the pores of the SiO2 core through RF preloading and hydrothermal treatment. The metallic MoS2 shell can directly transmit electric current from bacteria to an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode and acts as a conductive gate for electron flow into RF-based CQDs embedded in a semiconductive silica core, thus temporarily trapping electrons (charging) during shuttling. When these MoS2/CQD-based nanoshuttles were deposited on an ITO electrode, the loaded electrons were transferred from redox-active CQDs across the MoS2 shell to the electrode for discharging. Notedly, the RF-carbonized CQD inside the NP showed a unique electron retention feature that improves the electron-loaded density of NP as an electron nanoshuttle. The resulting CQD-embedded SiO2@MoS2 NPs were subsequently used as an electron nanoshuttle and a conductive biofilm scaffold in a microbial electrochemical reactor to facilitate long-distance indirect EET and direct EET, revealing a considerable increase in the bioelectricity generation.

Ethyl acetate (EA, C4H8O2, 99%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, C8H20O4Si, 98%), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4, 99%), and RF (C17H20N4O6, 98%) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, C19H42NBr, 96%) was purchased from Bio Basic. Ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3, 28%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 99.5%), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.5%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), and 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)− 1-piperazinyl]ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES, C8H18N2O4S, 99%) were purchased from Showa. Thiourea (CH4N2S, 99%), mercaptopropyltriethoxysilane (MPTES, 98%), and Luria–Bertani (LB) broth were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99%) was purchased from Aencore. 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Biotium. Yeast extract was purchased from Condalab. Water purified using a Milli-Q Synergy system was used throughout the study.

In this study, mesoporous SiO2 NPs were synthesized using the sol–gel method as follows. First, 0.24 g of CTAB was dissolved in 140 mL of distilled water, and 10 mL of EA and 4.0 mL of 28% NH3 were then added to the solution at 60 °C under gentle stirring. Moreover, 450 µL of TEOS was added to 4.55 mL of EtOH, and the mixture was then added dropwise to the aforementioned solution. After 6 h, the colloid was centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min to collect the SiO2 NPs, which were then redispersed in EtOH. Finally, the SiO2 NPs were washed at least twice to obtain EtOH-washed SiO2 NPs. For the acid-treated SiO2 NPs, the pre-synthesized SiO2 NPs were dispersed in 2 mL of EtOH containing 0.01 M HCl under stirring for 24 h, after which the aforementioned centrifugation and washing processes were conducted.

In general, to achieve MoS2 shell formation on the surface of SiO2 NPs, the thiol group on SiO2 NPs is crucial for enhancing the affinity between MoS2 and SiO2. In this study, to graft thiol groups onto SiO2 NPs, surface modification of SiO2 NPs was performed using MPTES. In brief, 150 µL of MPTES was added to 2 mL of an EtOH solution containing 0.02 g of SiO2 NPs under gentle stirring for 1 h. The product of the thiol-exposed SiO2 NPs (SiO2-SH NPs) was then collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol at least three times. Subsequently, 0.02 g of the SiO2-SH NPs was mixed with 0.05 g of thiourea and 0.05 g of sodium molybdate, after which 13.25 mL of 0.233 M HClaq was added to the mixture under stirring for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 24 h, which resulted in the formation of core-shell SiO2@MoS2 NPs. Finally, the product was collected, washed, and centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and then dispersed in ethanol at least three times.

A hydrothermal method was used to synthesize MoS2 nanosheets. In brief, 0.05 g of thiourea and 0.05 g of sodium molybdate were mixed, after which 13.25 mL of 0.233 M HClaq was added to the mixture under stirring for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to a furnace (CWF1300; Carbolite Gero, Hope, UK) at 180 °C for 24 h, which resulted in the formation of MoS2 nanosheets. Finally, the product was collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol. This process was repeated at least three times to purify the MoS2 nanosheets.

In this study, RF was selected as the carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs. Given the mesoporous nature of SiO2 NPs, RF was easily embedded into the SiO2 core by adding 0.2, 2, 5, and 20 mg of RF to 3 mL of an EtOH solution containing 0.02 g of SiO2 NPs under vigorous stirring for 24 h. After the reaction, the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs were collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol at least three times. Afterward, the surface modification was conducted on the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs by using MPTES to form RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs. Subsequently, 0.02 g of the RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs was mixed with 0.05 g of thiourea and 0.05 g of sodium molybdate, after which 13.25 mL of 0.233 M HClaq was added to the mixture under stirring for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 24 h, which resulted in the SiO2 @MoS2 NPs production via MoS2 shell formation on the surface of the SiO2 core and RF carbonization into N-doping CQD. Finally, the product was collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol. This process was repeated at least three times to purify the NPs.

This study applied a hydrothermal method for CQDs preparation by referring to the previous reports [40,41]. 0.005 g of RF was dissolved in 13.25 mL of water and was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 24 h, which resulted in the formation of RF-based CQDs. These CQDs were directly utilized without purification.

First, 5 mg of RF was added to 3 mL of an EtOH solution containing 0.02 g of SiO2 NPs under vigorous stirring for 24 h. After the reaction, the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs were collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol at least three times. Afterward, the surface modification was conducted on the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs by using MPTES to form RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs. Subsequently, 0.02 g of the RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs was dispersed in 13.25 mL of 0.233 M HClaq and was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 24 h, which resulted in the formation of CQDs-embedded SiO2 NPs. The HCl presence in the mentioned reaction is for avoiding the hydrolysis of silica NPs. Finally, the product was collected, washed, and centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and then dispersed in ethanol. This process was repeated at least three times to purify the NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 aggregates by two-step method
ce:section.ce:para: 0.02 g of the CQDs@SiO2 NPs was mixed with 0.05 g of thiourea and 0.05 g of sodium molybdate, after which 13.25 mL of 0.233 M HClaq was added to the mixture under stirring for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the product was collected, washed, centrifuged at 14 000 rpm for 5 min, and dispersed in ethanol. This process was repeated at least three times to purify the NPs.

In this study, S. oneidensis MR-1, which is an exoelectrogenic bacterial strain obtained from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center, was grown in colonies at 30 °C on LB agar plates. To conduct experiments in a liquid-phase system and at well-controlled bacterial concentrations, a single colony of S. oneidensis MR-1 was selected and incubated in 10 mL of LB medium under shaking at 30 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the bacterial pellet was collected through centrifugation at 4200 rpm and 4 °C for 10 min. The cells were then redispersed into a sterilized defined medium (DM) and washed at least twice to minimize the effect of residual LB in the electrochemical experiments. The optical density of the S. oneidensis MR-1 suspension was then determined at 600 nm (OD600) by using ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) measurements (Specord 200 Plus; Analytik Jena, Jena, Germany). The sterilized DM contained NaHCO3 (2.5 g/L), CaCl2 (0.08 g/L), NH4Cl (1 g/L), MgCl2 (0.2 g/L), NaCl (10 g/L), HEPES (7.2 g/L), and yeast extract (0.5 g/L). Engineered S. oneidensis MR-1 ΔOmcA/MtrC was obtained from American type culture collection (ATCC). The LB medium containing kanamycin (50 mg/mL) was applied for the culture of engineered S. oneidensis MR-1 ΔOmcA/MtrC.

The electrochemical system used in this study was composed of a chamber reactor, a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum wire counter electrode, and ITO glass (3 cm2). An anaerobic environment was established by adding 6.5 mL of sterilized DM and 10 mM lactate to the chamber and then purging the chamber with nitrogen for 15 min. Subsequently, 0.2 mL of S. oneidensis MR-1 suspension was added to adjust the final OD600 value to 0.1. Single-potential chronoamperometry (CA) measurements of the electrochemical cells were then performed using a potentiostat (VSP-300; BioLogic, Seyssinet-Pariset, France) at an electrode potential of 0.2 V versus a silver pseudo-reference electrode. After 10 min of measurement, 1 mg of the nanomaterial (which contained SiO2@MoS2 NPs, MoS2 nanosheets, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs) suspended in 0.2 mL of DM medium was added to the chamber. The additional differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were then conducted under the following conditions: a step height of 5 mV, a pulse height of 50 mV, a pulse width of 300 ms. Finally, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted to assess the electrochemical stability of SiO2@MoS2 NPs and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs and the redox activity of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs with bacteria under the following conditions: a scan range of − 0.4 to 0.8 V, a scan rate of 20 mV/s, and 20 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a depicts the preparation method for electroactive core–shell nanocomposites used to enhance the performance of MFCs. Nonconductive mesoporous SiO2 NPs were selected as the core and then covered with highly conductive MoS2 to form core–shell NPs. Monodispersed SiO2 NPs (205.2 ± 10.2 nm) were prepared through a sol–gel reaction (Fig. 1b). Given the mesoporous nature of SiO2 cores, directly loading a redox material within pores is regarded as the most intuitive method for enhancing the overall conductivity of NPs. The residual CTAB in the pores directly affected the molecular cargo loading; therefore, additional evaluations of porosity and surface area analysis were conducted for SiO2 NPs without purification and with ethanol and acid extractions [42,43]. BET analysis indicated that the surface area and pore volume of the EtOH-washed and acid-treated SiO2 NPs were considerably higher than those of the untreated SiO2 NPs, which indicated that CTAB was efficiently removed from the mesopores of the EtOH-washed and acid-treated SiO2 NPs (Figs. 1g and S1–4; see the detailed discussion in the supporting information). In this study, RF, which has excellent redox properties, was used to prepare RF-loaded SiO2 NPs (Fig. 1c). Unexpectedly, the EtOH-washed SiO2 NPs exhibited higher loading efficiency for 5 mg of RF than did the acid-treated SiO2 NPs (Fig. S5 and S6; see the detailed discussion in the supporting information); therefore, the EtOH-washed SiO2 NPs were selected for subsequent experiments. The successful loading of RF into mesoporous SiO2 NPs was then confirmed through Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, optical analysis, BET measurements, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). FTIR spectroscopy analysis revealed characteristic signals of SiO2 NPs at 1100 and 1250 cm−1 (Fig. 1h). Multiple characteristic vibration signals of RF, including those corresponding to CC and CO, were observed for the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs, which indicated successful RF loading (Fig. 1h). Moreover, NPs exhibited a substantial color change after yellow RF loading (Fig. 1i). The UV–Vis spectrum of the RF-loaded SiO2 NPs contained four absorbance peaks of RF at 224, 273, 350, and 451 nm (Fig. 1j). The results of BET analysis indicated that the surface area and pore volume of the EtOH-washed SiO2 NPs decreased considerably after RF loading, which implied that RF adsorption resulted in pore blocking (Fig. 1g). Through TGA, the relative weight percentages of CTAB and RF in RF-loaded SiO2 NPs were determined to be 8.0% and 31.0%, respectively (Fig. 1k).

The surface of RF-loaded SiO2 NPs was then modified using MPTES through another sol–gel reaction, which enabled the grafting of the thiol group (–SH) and considerably increased the affinity of silica to transition metals. However, no morphological changes were observed after the surface modification of the NPs by MPTES (Fig. 1d). Nevertheless, a substantial difference in zeta potential was observed between the original SiO2 NPs (−31.9 ± 3.4 mV) and the thiol-exposed RF-loaded SiO2 NPs (RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs, −52.6 ± 4.3 mV), which indicated successful surface thiolation (Fig. 1l). An additional vibration signal corresponding to S–H at 2566 cm−1 was detected only in the spectrum of SiO2-SH NPs, which indicated the successful exposure of thiol groups on the SiO2 core (Fig. 1h). Such exposure is essential for the subsequent deposition of MoS2. Under acidic conditions, the hydrothermal treatment of a molybdenum precursor containing thiourea as the sulfur source effectively generated MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. S7 and S8). In a previous study, RF was used as the carbon source for the formation of N-doped CQDs through hydrothermal treatment [40]. When RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs were added to the reaction and the aforementioned hydrothermal process was performed, the RF inside the pores of silica was carbonized to form CQDs [41]. Moreover, MoS2 was formed and deposited on the core surface to form CQD-embedded SiO2@MoS2 (SiO2-CQDs@MoS2) NPs because of the strong thiol-induced affinity at the SiO2–MoS2 interface. Compared to the two-step method divided by RF carbonization and MoS2 formation, the advantages of the one-step method are not only to considerably reduce the synthesis time, energy consumption, and operation complexity but also to produce monodispersed NPs after the hydrothermal reaction (Fig. S9a). When RF loading was not conducted, SiO2@MoS2 NPs were obtained (see Fig. S10 for the related characterization). A clear increase in the absorbance of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs was observed in the UV–Vis range, which indicated the presence of CQDs (Fig. 1j). No representative RF absorbance was detected for the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs, and negligible RF-involved weight loss was noted for these NPs in TGA. These results suggested that the RF inside the SiO2 NPs completely transformed into CQDs during the thermal treatment (Fig. 1j and k). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed a marginal increase in particle size and considerable variations in surface roughness following the formation of MoS2 (Fig. 1e). Similarly, a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of SiO2@MoS2 NPs revealed a yarn-ball-like morphology with a rough surface, which indicated the formation of a MoS2 shell through the deposition of two-dimensional MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. 1f). The formation of a MoS2 shell on the NPs resulted in them having a high volume-to-surface-area ratio, which increased their potential electrochemical activity (Fig. 1g). However, without prethiolation, no MoS2 shell was successfully formed around the original SiO2 NPs (Fig. S11).

For better understanding the role of CQDs in NP, RF-loaded SiO2 NPs prepared using different RF quantities (0, 0.2, 2, 5, and 20 mg) were used to produce SiO2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs0.2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs20@MoS2 NPs, all of which exhibited a consistent morphology and size distribution ( Fig. 2a,b). Raman analysis of these SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs revealed characteristic D- and G-band signals with RF-quantity-dependent intensities, which suggested the presence of CQDs (Fig. 2c) [40]. CQD-related Raman signals were also detected for SiO2@MoS2 NPs, which implied that some of the residual CTAB in the pores transformed into the CTAB-based CQDs [44]. High-resolution TEM with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was performed for SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs on a carbon-film-free copper grid. This analysis revealed that the aforementioned NPs had a yarn-ball-like morphology similar to that of the SiO2@MoS2 NPs. Moreover, the element distribution of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs indicated that they had a distinct core–shell element distribution, with Si and O being observed in the core (in SiO2) and Mo and S being observed in the shell (MoS2). These NPs also had homogenous C and N distributions (Fig. 2d). In the shell region of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, a lattice structure of MoS2 with lattice planes of (100) and (110) was detected (Fig. 2e). In addition, the X-ray diffraction pattern of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs revealed a well-defined MoS2 crystalline structure and characteristic SiO2 signal (Fig. 2f). Similarly, the FTIR spectrum of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs exhibited characteristic peaks at 1638 and 471 cm−1, which correspond to the vibration of the CC and Mo–S bonds, respectively (Fig. 1h). The vibration peaks observed at 658 cm−1, which were associated with Mo–O bond, indicated the formation of oxides on the surface of the NPs. The innate luminescence feature of CQDs was detected for the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs to prove the carbonization of RF (Fig. 2g). In addition, RF-based CQDs were identified in the TEM image showing a homogenous size distribution (4.6 ± 0.7 nm) that also reflects the putative size of CQDs in SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs (Fig. S12a). The additional optical characterizations of CQDs indicated the complete carbonization of RF into CQD (Fig. S12b-d). A remarkable N signal was found in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, which indicated that RF was transformed into N-doped CQDs with potential electron transfer ability (Fig. 2h) [40]. No N signal was observed for the SiO2@MoS2 NPs, which indicated that RF was the primary nitrogen source (Fig. S13). No signal of S-doping in CQDs, which usually appears at 167–169 eV, was detected for the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs; thus, MPTES did not transform into S-doped CQDs (Fig. S14) [45]. Moreover, CV measurements revealed that the SiO2@MoS2 and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs had a reversible redox state in a potential range of − 0.4 to 0.8 V over 20 cycles; thus, these NPs had excellent electrochemical stability (Fig. 2i). A considerable delay in oxidative current generation was observed for the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, which revealed the unique electron retention by the N-doped CQDs in these NPs (Fig. 2i). Finally, the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs did not affect the bacterial growth rate over 1 day; thus, these NPs have adequate bacterial compatibility for future applications of MFCs (Fig. 2j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical action of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs in MFCs based on S. oneidensis MR-1
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a closed-chamber electrochemical reactor was used to detect bioelectricity. On the basis of several extensive studies highlighting the well-characterized EET mechanism of S. oneidensis MR-1, this strain was used as an exoelectrogenic bacterial model in the proposed system [5,6]. Commonly used EET strategies involve using nanomaterial-modified working electrodes and inoculating bacteria with nanomaterials. However, in contrast to the aforementioned strategies, conductive NPs were directly injected as a suspended electron shuttle in a buffer in the present study to facilitate potential indirect long-distance EET from bacteria to an ITO electrode (Fig. S15). During the CA measurements, the electron production capacity of S. oneidensis MR-1 was easily monitored. The current production gradually increased with time, with a maximum current (I
c ) of 0.003 mA at 24 h ( Fig. 3a). After the SiO2@MoS2 nanoshuttles were injected into the reactor, considerable current amplification was observed, with a maximum I
c value of 0.006 mA at 5 h and continuous high current production until 24 h (Fig. 3a). At the end of the measurement phase, SEM imaging revealed that bacterial adhesion and SiO2@MoS2 nanoshuttle deposition occurred on the surface of the ITO electrode, which indicated an additional electron transport pathway for direct EET through biofilm formation (Fig. S16). The limited current produced by S. oneidensis MR-1 with MoS2 nanosheets and SiO2@MoS2 NPs is insufficient for actual applications of MFCs (Fig. 3a). Moreover, the CQD-embedded SiO2 (CQDs@SiO2) NPs and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 aggregates were respectively prepared by carbonizing RF-loaded SiO2-SH NPs and the two-step method (see Figs. S9a and S17 for the related characterization), which also showed a limited enhancement of bioelectricity production (Figs. S9b and S18). Excitingly, the addition of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs to S. oneidensis MR-1 resulted in a 10-fold increase in current generation after 24 h (Fig. 3b). No current production was detected from the SiO2@MoS2 NPs and SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs without bacteria (Fig. 3a and b). Additional electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted, showing a greater slope of SiO2 than that of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, demonstrating that a considerable reduction in impedance was obtained after CQD and MoS2 modification of the SiO2 core (Fig. S19) [46]. The aforementioned results indicate that MoS2/CQDs-based NPs can increase the systemic conductivity of MFCs by enhancing the electron transport efficiency, thereby increasing the current production.

Two representative outer membrane proteins MtrC and OmcA, located on the surface of S. oneidensis MR-1, act critically for electron transport to extracellular electron acceptors in the Mtr EET pathway [47]. Engineered S. oneidensis MR-1 ΔOmcA/MtrC lacking MtrC and OmcA was performed by removing the genes SO1778 and SO1779 from the wild type and thus obstructed EET, which acted as the EET-deficient group to be tested in the proposed system [48]. The results revealed a limited increase in the current generation in this mutant strain with the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, which suggested that the c-Cyt MtrC and OmcA involved in EET played a critical role in transferring metabolic electrons from the cell to the surrounding SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs (Fig. 3b). In general, outer-membrane complexes can bind with electron shuttles, like the OmcA-RF pair, that promote the direct and indirect EET of S. oneidensis MR-1 [49]. In addition, the strong current amplification observed with the SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs indicates that the high conductivity of these NPs and the rapid electron transport between outer-membrane proteins and these NPs enhance the performance of MFCs.

Subsequently, the current production in an MFC under the addition of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs prepared using different quantities of preloaded RF was evaluated. A gradual improvement in bioelectricity generation was observed from SiO2@MoS2 NPs to SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs. Similar electricity generation performance was achieved with the SiO2-CQDs2@MoS2 NPs, SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs, and SiO2-CQDs20@MoS2 NPs (Fig. 3c). The aforementioned results are in agreement with those of Raman analysis (Fig. 2c) and indicate that the quantity of the RF-based CQDs affected the EET to a certain extent. CV analysis indicated that the generated current increased with an increase in the CQD quantity of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs (Fig. 3d). The quantity of the RF-based CQDs in NPs also affected the delay condition in oxidative current production, which indicates that metabolic electrons were temporarily trapped in these NPs during shuttling (Fig. 3d). Compared with the NPs’ CV profiles, a relatively weak electrochemical response from the bacteria was obtained, implying the high impedance or poor affinity between the electroactive membrane protein of S. oneidensis MR-1 and the ITO electrode, and NP making it easier to transfer the loaded electron to the ITO electrode pulsed at positive potential (Fig. S20).

Additional differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was applied to distinguish the types of electron sources achieved on the ITO electrode and their relative contributions to the present system (Fig. 3e). A peak was observed at 0.22 V for the electrochemical system with S. oneidensis MR-1 alone, which indicated that direct EET was caused by bacterial adhesion onto the ITO electrode. The SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs alone displayed a weak oxidative current peak at 0.06 V. Interestingly, increased current intensities at 0.06 V were observed in S. oneidensis MR-1 with the presence of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs, thereby indicating an electrochemical interaction between them, which increased the electron-loading capacity of the nanoshuttles. Notably, the significantly enhanced current signal was observed at 0.22 V, which represented a potential direct EET pathway contributed by the NP-bound bacteria in the electrochemical system [49]. Moreover, the electron transfer rate constant (k) of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs was estimated as 2.9 × 10−5 cm s−1, obtained by additional CV measurements at different scan rates (60, 100, 140, 180, and 220 mV/s) and further calculation (Fig. S21; please refer to the supporting information for the related description and calculation method) [50,51].

XPS analysis of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs was performed before and after electrochemical treatment to determine the compositional changes that occurred because of repeated charging and discharging (Fig. 3f and g). Interestingly, the peaks of Mo(IV) and Mo(VI), which were contributed by the 1 T-phase MoS2 shell and thin surface oxide layer of MoO3, respectively, were obtained before electrochemical treatment (Fig. 3f). After electrochemical treatment, the signal of Mo(IV) remained constant, which indicated the high stability of 1 T-phase MoS2 [52]. However, the disappearance of the Mo(VI) peak for the aforementioned NPs after electron transport indicated the MoO3 (with positive reduction potential) on their surface was converted into electrochemically inactive MoO2 (with negative reduction potential) [53,54]. The aforementioned results confirm that SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs with excellent electrochemical stability can be applied in MFCs for considerably increasing electricity production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NP–promoted EET enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the ability of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs to enhance electron transfer, the roles of N-doped CQDs and MoS2 in the nanocomposite were further explored. A cheap, common, and N-free carbon source, namely citrate, was used to replace RF in material preparation to produce SiO2-CQDs(citrate)@MoS2 NPs. The size and morphology of these NPs were not considerably different from those of the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs, as indicated by TEM observations ( Fig. 4a). In the XPS analysis of the SiO2-CQDs(citrate)@MoS2 NPs, no N signal was obtained, which indicated the absence of N-doped CQDs in these NPs (Fig. 4b). The bioelectricity output obtained with the SiO2-CQDs(citrate)@MoS2 NPs was only one-fourth that obtained with the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs (Fig. 4c). This result implies that the redox-active RF-based CQDs in a nanoshuttle might act as an electron sink for rapidly receiving numerous metabolic electrons from bacteria and for efficiently transferring the loaded electrons to the electrode in an MFC system. In addition, SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs with thin and thick MoS2 shells were fabricated by adjusting the hydrothermal treatment time to 12 and 36 h, respectively (Fig. 4d and e). As expected, 12 h-hydrothermal-treated SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NP showed a relatively low current generation, possibly due to hindering electron transport in its incomplete MoS2 shell (Fig. 4f). A limited increase in bioelectricity generation was achieved with the NPs treated over 36 h, which indicates that an excessively thick MoS2 shell might weaken the EET enhancement effect of N-doped CQDs (Fig. 4f). SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs were respectively prepared through hydrothermal treatments at 140 and 220 ℃, showing either no MoS2 shell formation or considerable damage to the silica core. (Fig. S22). On the basis of all the experimental results of this study, a possible mechanism was identified for the enhancement of EET in S. oneidensis MR-1–based MFC systems by SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs (Fig. 4g). Direct contact between MtrCAB/OmcA complex and a metallic MoS2 shell with a vacant electron level of − 0.04 eV is essential for metabolic electron transport from bacteria to a nanoshuttle [55]. A semiconductor silica core has a relatively high conduction band potential of − 4.75 eV, which limits the interior conductivity of SiO2@MoS2 NPs [56,57]. The embedding of RF-based CQDs into silica mesopores results in the efficient conversion of an insulated silica core into a conductive electron sink for charging, thus maximizing electron transport during shuttling. Finally, the loaded electrons in nanoshuttles are discharged and then transferred to an electrode at a relatively low potential. Overall, this additional nanoshuttle-mediated EET pathway in MFC systems can substantially improve the electron transfer efficiency between bacteria and electrodes for enhancing bioelectricity generation. The results of nanoshuttle concentrations-dependent and bacteria-free measurements can further support this possible mechanism that the nanoshuttle acts as a conductive shortcut for metabolic electron transport between S. oneidensis MR-1 and the electrode (Figs. S23 and S24).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoshuttle-mediated indirect and direct EET in MFCs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, conductive NPs were used as electron shuttles to improve the performance of MFCs. This idea was inspired by the natural electron shuttle RF, which is secreted by S. oneidensis MR-1, and redox-active Fe3O4 NPs, which are used for interbacterial electron transfer [25]. In previous studies, conductive nanomaterials such as MoS2 nanosheets and MoS2-based nanocomposites have been used as anode or cathode materials to improve the electrochemical activity of cells (Table S1) [10–15]. The inoculation of conductive NP-containing biofilms is an excellent method for enhancing EET; however, the preculture process is time-consuming [16,17]. In the present study, SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs were used to coat an ITO working electrode during a current enhancement test for an S. oneidensis MR-1–based MFC system ( Fig. 5a). However, no current was detected in the thick coating of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs because of the limited electron transport within the multiple layers of the NPs (Fig. S25). The dense bacteria adhesion on the SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs-coated electrode was obtained to rule out the possibility of poor affinity between the cells and electrode surface to inhibit current production (Fig. S26). When the quantity of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs was reduced to prepare a thinly coated electrode, the electricity amplification effect depended on passive cell adhesion and biofilm formation, which resulted in lower current production than that achieved with the proposed nanoshuttle approach (Fig. 5a and S27). In addition, a comparison conducted under the same number of molecular shuttles and nanoscale electron shuttles revealed that the Brownian-motion-driven current enhancement effect of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 nanoshuttles was considerably stronger than the current enhancement effect observed with diffusion-driven RF. This result indicates the potential advantages of NPs, including their favorable mass transfer effect, high conductivity, and high specific surface area (Fig. S27; please refer to the supporting information for the related description and calculation method). Because SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 nanoshuttles are considerably larger than CQDs, another novel pathway of feeding NPs to S. oneidensis MR-1 for electricity enhancement was excluded [35,36]. The stable redox activity of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs at a wide potential range and their high bacterial compatibility allowed them to serve as conductive shuttles for electron transport cycling (Fig. 2i and j).

The biofilm gradually generated on the surface of the ITO electrode after electrochemical treatment contained adhesive SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs, which suggested an additional role of SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs as a conductive scaffold inside the biofilm; this scaffold contributed to direct short-distance EET. Additional evidence of the NPs-doped biofilm formation was obtained by observation in SEM and fluorescence images (Fig. 5b and S16). The image of biofilm under the DAPI channel showed clear DAPI-stained S. oneidensis MR-1 bacilli (red arrow) and an unspecific DAPI-contaminated extracellular matrix (yellow arrow), thus evidencing the feasibility of DAPI dye in staining biofilm. The SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs have an innate fluorescence feature with a broad emission range from 400 to 550 nm, thus being visualized under the DAPI and FITC channels. In the FITC channel, no fluorescence signal was detected in S. oneidensis MR-1 biofilm to indicate the absence of NP. In the fluorescence image of the NPs-doped biofilm, the overlapped position of NPs-bound bacteria (red arrows) within the surrounding matrix (yellow arrow) was obtained, implying the NPs-doped biofilm formation. An additional test that involved stirring every hour to maintain the suspension of nanoshuttles for indirect EET alone indicated that the bioelectricity generation decreased considerably than no stirring condition. Thus, nanoshuttle-doped biofilm formation had a non-negligible contribution in enhancing the direct EET in the MFC (Fig. 5c). Overall, in the MFC system, SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs mainly served as electron nanoshuttles for indirect long-distance EET in the initial stage and synergistically increased the conductivity of the electrode for direct short-distance EET in the late stage, which resulted in a considerable increase in the current output of the system (Fig. 5d). The feasibility of SiO2-CQDs5@MoS2 NPs in a simplified air-MFC was tested, and the voltage increased by 20% after NP injection (Fig. S28).

In this study, core–shell SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs were successfully prepared. Thiolation of the SiO2 surface facilitated its interaction with metallic MoS2 nanosheets, which allowed for the production of nanoscale yarn-ball-like NPs with excellent conductivity. Redox-active RF-based CQDs were then embedded into the mesopores of the SiO2 core to increase the conductivity considerably and achieve enhanced electron trapping ability. A possible mechanism was identified for the enhancement of EET by SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs in an S. oneidensis MR-1–based MFC system. The results indicated that the prepared SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 NPs had high electrochemical stability and bacterial biocompatibility. As a proof of concept, substantial current amplification was observed when SiO2-CQDs@MoS2 nanoshuttles were used in the proposed S. oneidensis MR-1 electrochemical system, which indicated a large improvement in performance compared with that achieved with RF, MoS2 nanosheets, and SiO2-CQDs@MoS2-coated electrodes. Moreover, an alternative strategy of using conductive nanoshuttles for enhancing indirect and direct EET was evaluated, thereby laying the foundation for the development of advanced MFCs.

Li Wei-Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wang Wen-Jyun: Validation. Peng Hung-Hsi: Validation. Kuo Yi-Ho: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Hsu Ming-Chien: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yi-Ho Kuo is a master's student in the Department of Medicine and Applied Chemistry at Kaohsiung Medical University. His research focuses on novel nanomaterial design, preparation, and applications in medical and microbial electrochemical fields. In this study, he carried out all experiments and data analyses

Ming-Chien Hsu was a research assistant in the Innovative Nanomedicine Laboratory at Kaohsiung Medical University. Her assistance in the present work included bacterial culture and electrochemical analysis.

Wen-Jyun Wang is a research assistant in the Innovative Nanomedicine Laboratory at Kaohsiung Medical University. Her contribution to the present work included the characterization of nanoparticles, electrochemical analysis, and biofilm observation.
Hung-Hsi Peng is an undergrad in the Department of Medicine and Applied Chemistry at Kaohsiung Medical University. In this study, he mainly assisted in the nanoparticle preparation.

Dr. Wei-Peng Li, an Assistant Professor and Yushan Young Scholar at Kaohsiung Medical University, heads the Innovative Nanomedicine Laboratory. His expertise lies in groundbreaking nanomaterials and microbial electrochemistry to address multifaceted challenges. After earning his Ph.D. in Chemistry from National Cheng Kung University in 2015, he explored nanotechnology applications in oncology, therapy, and wound healing. His recent collaborations merged microbial electrochemistry with nanotechnology, forming a pioneering research group at KMU.
